sources.data: Goblin Slayer is a dark fantasy anime series based on the light novel by Kumo Kagyu. The story follows a lone adventurer known only as 'Goblin Slayer' who has dedicated his life to the extermination of goblins after they destroyed his village and murdered his family when he was a child. In this world, goblins are often dismissed as weak, low-level monsters by veteran adventurers, but Goblin Slayer knows they are cunning, prolific, and deadly when underestimated. The anime is notorious for its graphic violence in the first episode, where a party of rookie adventurers is brutally massacred by goblins. Goblin Slayer operates on a simple philosophy: goblins are the only evil that matters, and he will use any means—poison, traps, burning oil, ambushes—to kill them. The series has spawned a movie (Goblin Slayer: Goblin's Crown) and a second season. It is produced by WHITE FOX studio.

Goblin Slayer: Goblin's Crown is a 2020 anime film continuing the Goblin Slayer series. The story adapts the fifth volume of the light novel, where Goblin Slayer and his party accept a quest from a noble to rescue a noblewoman captured by goblins in a frozen northern region. The goblins in this film are led by a unique 'Goblin Lord' variant called a 'Goblin Paladin', which has leadership abilities and commands a large horde. The film explores more of the world's lore about goblin reproduction and society, while delivering the same gritty tactical combat the series is known for.

Goblin Slayer II is the 2023 sequel season continuing the story of Goblin Slayer and his adventuring party. The second season delves deeper into the political landscape of the adventurer's guild and the wider world, while Goblin Slayer continues his obsessive crusade against goblins. New goblin types appear including goblin shamans with more sophisticated magic and goblins that have learned to domesticate wolves for mounted combat. The season also explores the psychological toll of Goblin Slayer's single-minded obsession, as his companions grow increasingly concerned about his inability to see goblins as anything but irredeemable monsters.

Goblin Is Very Strong (Goblin wa Machigai daga Tsuyoi) is a 2025 anime adaptation of the manga series. The premise is a comedic twist on the isekai genre: a salaryman is reincarnated in a fantasy world as a goblin, but while he appears to be a tiny, weak goblin, he possesses absurdly overpowered stats. The humor derives from the contrast between his goblin appearance and his reality-bending strength. In a genre where goblins are typically the weakest monsters that new adventurers practice on, this goblin protagonist turns the trope on its head by being unstoppably powerful while maintaining a goblin's mischievous personality.

Goblins appear across a wide spectrum of anime and manga, from serious dark fantasy to comedy. Notable appearances include: Goblin Slayer (the most famous dark take on goblins); Overlord (goblins as summoned monsters, including the Goblin General empowered by a magic horn); Re:Monster (a protagonist reincarnated as a goblin who builds a goblin empire); That Time I Got Reincarnated as a Slime (goblins and hobgoblins as early allies); Rokka no Yuusha (goblins as part of the monster army); The Rising of the Shield Hero (goblins as early-level enemies that grow into a threat); DanMachi (goblins appear in the dungeon as low-level monsters); Grimgar of Fantasy and Ash (realistic depiction of goblins as dangerous creatures that new adventurers struggle to defeat); Goblin Is Very Strong (comedic take on goblin reincarnation); and many isekai series where goblins serve as introductory enemies. The portrayal of goblins in anime typically inherits from their Western fantasy roots—small, green-skinned, crude, and often violent—but Japanese media has added unique twists such as goblin reincarnation (tensei) stories and the 'cute goblin girl' subgenre.

Goblins appear in various visual novels, most often as antagonists or monster enemies in fantasy settings. Notable VNs with goblin content include: Chaos;Head Noah and its sequels (part of the Science Adventure series, which features 'Gigalomaniacs' whose powers are influenced by goblin-related delusions); Eustia of the Tarnished Wings (a fantasy VN with goblin characters); Rance series (a long-running eroge franchise where goblins appear as enemy monsters); Evenicle (a fantasy RPG/VN hybrid where goblins appear as early-game enemies); Black Souls series (dark fantasy RPG-maker games with goblin encounters); The Testament of Sister New Devil (features goblins as summoned demons); and various Dungeons & Dragons-inspired Japanese visual novels where goblins feature as low-level dungeon inhabitants. In the VN space, goblins are typically portrayed as aggressive, tribal creatures that attack in groups, but some works subvert this by making goblin characters sympathetic or comedic.

Labyrinth (1986) is a musical fantasy film directed by Jim Henson and starring David Bowie as Jareth, the Goblin King. The story follows 16-year-old Sarah (Jennifer Connelly) who, in a moment of frustration, wishes that goblins would take away her baby brother Toby. The goblins—depicted through Jim Henson's masterful puppetry—swiftly obey, taking Toby to Jareth's castle in the center of a vast labyrinth. Sarah must navigate the maze of the Goblin Kingdom to rescue her brother before the clock strikes thirteen. Jareth himself is portrayed as a charismatic, androgynous, and manipulative ruler of the goblins, with shapeshifting powers and a magnetic presence. The goblins of Labyrinth are a diverse cast of bizarre, whimsical puppet creatures—some helpful, some obstructive, all distinctly Henson-esque. The film's goblins are not evil in the traditional sense but are mischievous servants of the Goblin King, bound to do his bidding. Bowie's Jareth became an iconic figure in pop culture, and the film's unique blend of puppetry, fantasy, and music has made it a cult classic. The depiction of the Goblin King draws from European folklore where goblin kings rule over subterranean courts of mischievous spirits.

Goblins in the Harry Potter universe are a highly intelligent, sentient non-human magical species who run the wizarding bank Gringotts. They are described as having swarthy faces, long fingers and feet, pointed beards, and a love for metalwork and money. The most prominent goblin characters include Griphook (who assists Harry, Ron, and Hermione in breaking into Gringotts in Deathly Hallows), Bogrod (a Gringotts goblin placed under the Imperius Curse), and Bill Weasley's co-worker at Gringotts. The relationship between wizards and goblins is historically tense—goblins resent wizard dominance and have a long history of rebellions, most famously the 18th-century goblin rebellion led by the Goblin King. Goblins operate their own legal system and have strict views on ownership: they believe the creator of an object retains rights to it even after sale, which is why they are concerned about magical artifacts made by goblins. In Harry Potter and the Deathly Hallows (film and book), the goblin concept of 'mastery' over magical objects—specifically the Sword of Gryffindor—plays a crucial role in the plot. The Harry Potter films portrayed goblins with distinctive prosthetic makeup, giving them elongated features and sharp teeth. J.K. Rowling's goblins have faced some criticism over the years for drawing on antisemitic tropes in their depiction (associated with banking, money, secret conspiracies, and physical stereotypes).

sources.source: IMDb - The Hobbit (Film Trilogy) - Goblins & Orcs
sources.data: In J.R.R. Tolkien's Middle-earth, goblins and orcs are essentially the same species, with 'goblin' being the more common term used in The Hobbit. The Hobbit (1937) features memorable goblin encounters: the Company of Thorin Oakenshield is captured by goblins in the Misty Mountains, where they confront the Great Goblin—a massive, brutal ruler of the mountain goblins. The goblins of the Misty Mountains use crude weapons and tools, torture their captives, and are terrified of the sword Orcrist (called 'Biter' by goblins). In Peter Jackson's film adaptations (2012-2014), the goblins are depicted as twisted, pale-skinned creatures with large eyes adapted to living in darkness. The Goblin King in the films is an enormous, grotesque creature voiced by Barry Humphries. After escaping the Misty Mountains via the Goblin Tunnels, the Company is pursued by goblins and Wargs during the classic 'Burning of the Trees' sequence. The Battle of Five Armies features a massive goblin army led by Bolg. Tolkien's goblins/orcs are perhaps the most influential depiction of goblins in modern fantasy literature, establishing the template of goblins as warlike, cruel, industrial-minded creatures who hate sunlight and breed in dark places.

Willow (1988) is a fantasy film directed by Ron Howard and produced by George Lucas, featuring a variety of goblin-like creatures. The story follows Willow Ufgood (Warwick Davis), a Nelwyn (a race of diminutive, peaceful farmers) who must protect a special baby from the evil Queen Bavmorda. While not called goblins directly, the Nelwyn share many characteristics with folklore's friendly house-goblin/brownie tradition. The film also features the character of Madmartigan (Val Kilmer), a rogue warrior who battles monstrous trolls and goblin-like creatures. The film's most famous goblin-like creatures include the two-headed Eborsisk dragon (a nod to film critics Ebert and Siskel) and the trolls of the Daikini forest. Willow established many fantasy creature tropes that would influence later depictions of goblins in cinema, particularly in showing that small, unassuming beings can be the heroes of their own stories.

The Gremlins films (1984, 1990) feature creatures whose name derives from an old English folklore term for mischievous goblin-like beings originally blamed for mechanical failures in RAF aircraft during World War I and II. The film's Mogwai—cute, furry creatures that transform into reptilian goblin-like monsters called Gremlins when exposed to water or fed after midnight—are a modern cinematic take on the goblin trickster archetype. In Gremlins 2: The New Batch, the Gremlins mutate into various specialized forms including a bat-Gremlin, a spider-Gremlin, an electric Gremlin, and a 'smart' Gremlin who speaks and wears glasses. Like traditional goblins, Gremlins are chaotic, destructive, and find joy in mayhem. They multiply rapidly, love causing technological disasters, and are ultimately defeated by sunlight—a weakness shared with many goblin traditions. The films tapped into the same folkloric vein as goblin mythology while creating an original American spin on the concept.

The Green Goblin is one of Spider-Man's most iconic villains, created by Stan Lee and Steve Ditko in 1964. Norman Osborn, a ruthless industrialist, gains superhuman strength and insanity after testing an experimental formula. He adopts the Green Goblin persona, using a goblin-themed costume, a glider, and pumpkin bombs. The name draws from the folklore goblin archetype—a trickster who delights in chaos. The Green Goblin has appeared in multiple Spider-Man films: Willem Dafoe portrayed him in Sam Raimi's Spider-Man (2002) and later in Spider-Man: No Way Home (2021); James Franco played Harry Osborn as a version of the New Goblin in Spider-Man 3 (2007); and Dane DeHaan portrayed a younger Harry Osborn as the Green Goblin in The Amazing Spider-Man 2 (2014). The Hobgoblin, a successor villain introduced in the comics, carries on the goblin legacy. The 'goblin' branding in Marvel represents the chaotic, trickster, and sinister elements of the folklore origin, adapted into a high-tech supervillain concept.

Goblins are one of the most common fantasy races in modern media, appearing across literature, gaming, film, and animation. Common tropes associated with goblins include: (1) The Cannon Fodder Trope—goblins are typically the weakest monsters that beginner heroes practice on, appearing in large numbers; (2) The Goblin King—a stronger, often more intelligent leader who commands goblin hordes; (3) Mischievous Tricksters—goblins love pranks, theft, and general chaos, sometimes harmless, sometimes deadly; (4) Industrial Saboteurs—from the RAF's 'gremlin' folklore to Warhammer's goblin inventors, they're associated with machinery breakdowns; (5) Goblins vs. Dwarves—a classic fantasy rivalry going back to Norse mythology and Tolkien; (6) Subterranean Dwellers—goblins live in caves, mines, and underground tunnels; (7) Rapid Reproduction—goblins often reproduce quickly and in large numbers, explaining why they're so numerous; (8) The Unlikely Hero—modern works increasingly feature goblins as protagonists rather than mere enemies, especially in Japanese isekai where humans are reincarnated as goblins and rise to power. Notable works that define modern goblin tropes include J.R.R. Tolkien's The Hobbit, Dungeons & Dragons, Jim Henson's Labyrinth, the Goblin Slayer series, J.K. Rowling's Harry Potter, Terry Pratchett's Discworld (where goblins are a marginalized race seeking justice), and the Warcraft universe (where goblins are capitalist inventors).

In Dungeons & Dragons, goblins were introduced in the original 1974 edition and have appeared in every edition since. In current 5th Edition lore, goblins are small, dark-skinned humanoids with sharp teeth, who dwell in caves, ruins, and other dark places. They are fiercely territorial but cowardly, preferring ambushes and overwhelming numbers to fair fights. Goblin culture is tribal and oppressive, ruled by the strongest—often a goblin boss, a hobgoblin, or a bugbear. Goblins worship a pantheon including Maglubiyet, the goblinoid god of war, who demands constant conquest. The D&D goblin has undergone significant evolution: early editions treated them as simple cannon fodder; later editions, particularly 5th Edition's Volo's Guide to Monsters and Monsters of the Multiverse, added depth to goblin society, culture, and even playable goblin characters. The 'goblin' creature type in D&D encompasses several varieties: the common goblin, the nimblewright-like variants, and goblin bosses with extra abilities. As a playable race (introduced in Volo's and updated in Monsters of the Multiverse), goblin player characters gain the 'Fury of the Small' ability, allowing them to deal extra damage to larger creatures—a fitting mechanical expression of the goblin's scrappy, underdog nature. The D&D goblin has heavily influenced digital RPGs including Baldur's Gate 3, where goblins feature prominently as early-game enemies with their own camp culture, priests worshipping the Absolute, and memorable NPCs.

Goblins in the Warcraft universe are green-skinned, cunning, and mercantile creatures driven by profit and invention. Originally enslaved by the trolldom of the jungle trolls, goblins won their freedom in a rebellion and established a capitalist society centered on the Trade Prince cartels. Warcraft goblins are defined by their entrepreneurial greed, reckless engineering, and use of 'goblin-tech'—unstable but effective machinery, explosives, and vehicles that tend to backfire spectacularly. In World of Warcraft, goblins became a playable race in the Cataclysm expansion (2010), with their starting zone on Kezan and the Lost Isles. Goblins choose between the four Trade Cartels: Bilgewater (the playable Horde faction aligned with the Bilgewater Cartel led by Trade Prince Gallywix, later replaced by Gazlowe), Steamwheedle (neutral entrepreneurs), Venture Company (aggressive industrialists), and Blackwater (racketeers). Notable goblin characters include Gallywix (the greedy former leader of the Bilgewater Cartel), Gazlowe (the more benevolent leader who takes over, known from Warcraft III's goblin merchant), and the goblin engineers responsible for inventions like the Goblin Shredder, the Zeppelin, and various bombs. Goblins in Warcraft are distinct from other fantasy goblins in that they are not primarily warriors but industrialists, inventors, and merchants—a take that emphasizes the 'gremlin/mischievous inventor' aspect of goblin folklore.

sources.source: Warhammer Fantasy - Goblin & Night Goblin Lore
sources.data: In Warhammer Fantasy, goblins are the most numerous Greenskin race, smaller than orcs but far more numerous and sneaky. They are brutal, cowardly, treacherous, and have a particular love for cruelty and torture. Goblins in Warhammer ride wolves (as Goblin Wolf Riders), pilot crude flying machines, and wielding weapons designed to be as unfair as possible—poisoned arrows, nets, and fanatics with spiked flails. Notable goblin variants include Night Goblins (from the mountains, dedicated to the mushroom god and using spooky moon imagery), Forest Goblins (spider-riding jungle dwellers), and the notorious Goblin Fanatics—goblins who spin huge spiked chains in a frenzy and are equally dangerous to enemies and allies. Warhammer goblins are led by powerful characters such as Skarsnik, the Night Goblin Warlord who once captured the dwarven stronghold of Karak Eight Peaks. The goblins of Warhammer represent the most comedic and treacherous interpretation of the folkloric goblin—they're cunning, cruel, cowardly, and always scheming to betray their orc allies for personal gain.

Goblins are one of the most iconic creature types in Magic: The Gathering, appearing in every color but most famously in Red. MTG goblins are characterized by their reckless aggression, lack of self-preservation, and tendency to explode. They follow a tribal theme where individual goblins are weak but multiply and buff each other rapidly. Classic goblin cards include Goblin Guide (a cheap aggressive creature that defined burn decks), Goblin Rabblemaster (a modern goblin lord that creates tokens), Krenko, Mob Boss (an explosive commander that doubles goblin tokens), Siege-Gang Commander (which turns goblins into direct damage), and Muxus, Goblin Grandee (a legendary goblin from the D&D crossover set Adventures in the Forgotten Realms). The goblins of MTG span multiple planes: the goblins of Dominaria (Sliv-Mender, the squee goblins who cannot die), the goblins of Ravnica (the Izzet League and the Rakdos cult both employ goblins in dangerous experiments and shows), the goblins of Tarkir (cunning and tribal), and the goblins of Kaladesh (inventive and energetic). The phrase 'Goblins are Red' has become a meme in Magic culture—Red's philosophy of emotion, impulse, chaos, and destruction perfectly embodies goblin nature. The tribe has remained competitive in various formats for decades.

GOBLIN is a prominent Japanese rock band formed in 2005 and signed to Being Inc. Their music has been featured as theme songs for numerous anime series, most notably Naruto Shippuden ('Utagoe Hikaru Genji' and others), Detective Conan, and various other anime. While 'goblin' here is a band name rather than a reference to folklore creatures, the band's adoption of the name contributes to goblin cultural presence in Japanese media. The band's energetic rock style and anime theme contributions have made the name 'Goblin' familiar to anime audiences worldwide, even if they're not thinking of folklore creatures.

Pathfinder goblins are small, green-skinned humanoids with enormous mouths full of sharp teeth, a hatred of writing and books (they believe reading steals ideas from your head), and a love of fire, dogs (which they ride), and loud noises. They speak Goblin with a distinctive high-pitched voice and are known for their catchphrase: 'GOBLINS EAT ANYTHING!' Pathfinder goblins are more comedic than their D&D counterparts, leaning into slapstick violence and absurd chaos. They fear horses (which they call 'horsenemies'), despise the written word, and believe the name of the first goblin was 'Zogmug'. Pathfinder made goblins a core playable ancestry (race) in its second edition, with options including goblin characters who become fire dancers (Flame Oracles), dog-riding cavaliers, or even goblin alchemists who love explosions. The Pathfinder goblin is arguably the most distinct and original take on the goblin archetype in tabletop gaming—turning them from simple low-level monsters into a chaotic, lovable, and memorable playable species.

In Terry Pratchett's Discworld series, goblins are introduced prominently in the novels Snuff (2011) and Raising Steam (2013). Discworld goblins are an oppressed, marginalized species living in caves and garbage dumps, feared and reviled by humans who consider them vermin. Pratchett subverts the traditional goblin stereotype through Commander Sam Vimes of the Ankh-Morpork City Watch, who discovers that goblins have a sophisticated culture, complex language, and unique talents—particularly an extraordinary sense of smell and a gift for metalworking that rivals dwarves. The goblins of Discworld are intelligent, misunderstood creatures who have been driven to the edges of society by prejudice. Their re-evaluation in the novels is a pointed commentary on racism, classism, and how societies dehumanize (or de-goblin-ize) marginalized groups. Pratchett's goblins represent one of the most thoughtful and humanistic takes on the goblin archetype in fantasy literature, transforming them from simple monsters into a metaphor for oppressed peoples.

sources.source: Goblin Mode - Oxford Word of the Year 2022
sources.data: In 2022, 'goblin mode' was named Oxford Word of the Year after a public vote. The term describes a type of behavior that is 'unapologetically self-indulgent, lazy, slovenly, or greedy, typically in a way that rejects social norms or expectations.' It went viral in early 2022 after a tweet featuring a doctored headline claiming Julia Fox had used the term. The expression resonated strongly during the COVID-19 pandemic era, when people embraced comfort over social performance and rejected the curated perfectionism of social media. The term's success demonstrates the enduring cultural power of goblin imagery—the goblin as a symbol of chaos, mischief, and rejection of social expectations. 'Goblin mode' represents the folkloric goblin's mischievous, rule-breaking nature adapted to modern self-care and anti-perfectionist discourse.
